label: Localized cosmetic rash without eye or anaphylaxis red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit a localized skin reaction from cosmetic exposure.
found: Your clinician diagnosed a localized cosmetic exposure skin reaction. We did not find anaphylaxis, mucosal involvement, skin infection, eye involvement, or another rash emergency today.
home: Avoid the suspected trigger if it is known.
Wash gently with mild soap and water, and avoid scrubbing the rash.
Use cool compresses, moisturizers, or coverings only as your clinician recommended.
meds: Use creams, ointments, itch medicine, or allergy medicine only if prescribed or recommended.
Do not use leftover steroid, antibiotic, or allergy medicine unless your clinician told you to.
return: Trouble breathing, throat tightness, swelling of the lips or tongue, fainting, or a bodywide reaction.
Fever, spreading redness, warmth, pus, red streaks, rapidly worsening rash, skin peeling, or mouth sores.
Eye pain, vision change, eyelid swelling, or symptoms not improving as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, dermatology, allergy, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of localized cosmetic exposure skin reaction.
No anaphylaxis, systemic allergic reaction, mucosal involvement, skin infection, severe medication reaction, eye involvement, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Anaphylaxis, airway symptoms, hypotension, systemic allergic reaction, mucosal lesions, severe cutaneous adverse reaction, skin infection, rapidly progressive rash, burn or chemical exposure, ocular symptoms or periorbital concern, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed dermatology/allergy plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports contact dermatitis framing from irritant or allergic exposures, trigger avoidance, gentle skin care, clinician-directed topical or allergy treatment, and escalation for severe allergic, infectious, ocular, or mucosal red flags. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed localized cosmetic exposure skin reaction with anaphylaxis, infection, mucosal, and severe-rash red flags absent.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of localized cosmetic exposure skin reaction
no infection, mucosal, or severe-rash red flags

label: Localized spider bite without infection or necrotic red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit a local skin reaction from a spider bite.
found: Your clinician diagnosed a localized spider bite. We did not find spreading infection, anaphylaxis, necrotic spider concern, scorpion sting concern, or another bite emergency today.
home: Wash the area gently with soap and water.
Use cool compresses for itching or swelling.
meds: Use itch or allergy medicines only if prescribed or recommended.
Use acetaminophen or ibuprofen only if you can take it safely and follow the label.
return: Trouble breathing, throat swelling, vomiting after a bite, fainting, or a bodywide reaction.
Spreading redness, pus, fever, red streaks, black skin, severe pain, or a rapidly worsening wound.
Symptoms that worsen or do not improve as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, dermatology, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of localized spider bite.

No anaphylaxis, systemic allergic reaction, secondary infection, necrotic spider or scorpion concern, tick-borne illness concern, snakebite concern, immunocompromised host, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Anaphylaxis, systemic allergic reaction, secondary infection, necrotic spider or scorpion concern, tick-borne illness concern, snakebite concern, immunocompromised host, or specialist-directed allergy/dermatology plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus insect bite material supports local bite care, itch control, and escalation for allergic reactions, infection, or systemic symptoms. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed localized spider bite with infection, anaphylaxis, necrotic spider, scorpion, tick-borne, and snakebite red flags absent.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of localized spider bite

label: Hand rubbing blister without infection or burn red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit an uncomplicated hand rubbing blister.
found: Your clinician diagnosed an uncomplicated hand rubbing blister. We did not find infection, burn injury, widespread blistering, or another emergency problem today.
home: Protect the blister from more rubbing or pressure.
Keep the area clean and dry unless your clinician told you something different.
Do not pop, cut, or peel the blister unless your clinician instructed you to.
meds: Use pain medicine only if prescribed or recommended, and follow the label.
Use creams, ointments, or antibiotics only if your clinician prescribed or recommended them.
return: Spreading redness, warmth, pus, red streaks, fever, or worsening swelling.
Skin peeling, mouth sores, widespread blisters, severe pain, or a blister from a burn or chemical exposure.
Numbness, weakness, color change, or symptoms not improving as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, wound care, or the ED if the blister is not healing or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated hand rubbing blister.
No infection, burn pathway, widespread blistering, medication reaction, mucosal involvement, high-risk host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Cellulitis, abscess, pus, open infected wound, fever, systemic illness, burn, chemical/electrical injury, widespread blistering, mucosal lesions, severe cutaneous adverse reaction concern, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed wound plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports blister framing, protection from rubbing, simple skin care, and escalation for infection, burns, widespread blistering, mucosal lesions, or severe skin reactions. This expansion is limited to clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated hand rubbing blister with infection, burn, high-risk skin, and red flags absent.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis of uncomplicated hand rubbing blister
no burn or severe skin reaction
